cards.text: To accept command line arguments, main function signature is: {{c1::int main(int argc, char* argv[])}} or {{c2::int main(int argc, char** argv)}}

cards.front: What does argc represent in main(int argc, char* argv[])?
cards.back: argc (argument count) is the number of command line arguments passed, including the program name itself. It's always at least 1.

cards.front: What does argv represent in main(int argc, char* argv[])?
cards.back: argv (argument vector) is an array of C-style strings containing the command line arguments. argv[0] is the program name, argv[1] is the first argument, etc.

cards.question:
If you run: ./program hello world
What are argc and argv values?
cards.code:
int main(int argc, char* argv[])
{
    // argc = ?
    // argv[0] = ?
    // argv[1] = ?
    // argv[2] = ?
}
cards.explanation: argc counts all arguments including program name. argv is zero-indexed with program name at index 0.

cards.text: Command line arguments are always passed as {{c1::C-style strings (char*)}}. To use them as numbers, you must {{c2::convert them}} using functions like std::stoi or std::atoi.

cards.question: How do you convert command line arguments to integers?
cards.code:
// Running: ./program 42 100
int main(int argc, char* argv[])
{
    // Convert argv[1] to int?
}
cards.answer:
int num1 = std::stoi(argv[1]);
int num2 = std::stoi(argv[2]);

Or using C-style:
int num1 = std::atoi(argv[1]);
cards.explanation: std::stoi (C++11) is preferred over atoi because it provides better error handling.

cards.front: What should you check before accessing argv[1], argv[2], etc.?
cards.back: Always check that argc is large enough. If argc <= 1, there are no user-provided arguments (only program name).

cards.question: What's the proper way to validate command line arguments?
cards.code:
int main(int argc, char* argv[])
{
    // Need 2 arguments from user
    // What should you check?
}
cards.explanation: Check argc first to ensure arguments exist. Use std::cerr for error messages. Return non-zero to indicate error.

cards.text: To use command line arguments as std::string, convert using: {{c1::std::string str(argv[1]);}} or {{c2::std::string str = argv[1];}}

cards.front: How do you pass a command line argument that contains spaces?
cards.back:
Enclose it in quotes: ./program "hello world"
This will be treated as a single argument in argv[1].

cards.concept: Common use cases for command line arguments
cards.explanation:
Command line arguments are commonly used for:
  • Specifying input/output files
  • Setting configuration options or flags
  • Controlling program behavior without recompiling
  • Batch processing and automation
  • Tool integration in build systems

cards.question: How do you iterate through all command line arguments?
cards.code:
int main(int argc, char* argv[])
{
    // Print all arguments
}
cards.answer:
for (int i = 0; i < argc; ++i)
{
    std::cout << "argv[" << i << "]: " << argv[i] << '\n';
}

Or skip program name:
for (int i = 1; i < argc; ++i)
cards.explanation: Loop from 0 to argc (exclusive). Start from index 1 if you want to skip the program name.
